quote: Just saw you were just looking into some details on solar for your home here in Williamsburg.
stewart_read: The rep opens with a solar frame on a Powerwall-only lead because the notes hadn't loaded before the call. This is a preparation failure, not a strategic choice. Wayne's immediate correction ('No. I never wanted solar. That's not what I asked for.') is the direct consequence. The rep recovers at 00:48 by confirming Powerwall availability, but the opening credibility cost is real and unrepaired.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was using the solar intro as a legitimacy anchor before pivoting to Powerwall. Rejected — the rep's own admission at 00:40 ('It was just taking a sec to load the notes here') confirms this was a preparation gap, not a deliberate framing choice.
coaching_implication: Manager should establish a floor norm: notes loaded and read before the dial connects. This call cost the rep 40 seconds of credibility repair that Wayne never fully forgave. Pull this clip as the 'what happens when you dial cold' example.

quote: Oh, it's in the request. Don't you guys get that information when I put in the request?
stewart_read: Wayne is questioning Ion's internal data competence — specifically whether the rep should already know what he requested. This is not a contact-origin suspicion question; it's a process-credibility challenge. The rep's response ('Yeah. It was just taking a sec to load the notes here.') is honest but exposes the preparation failure rather than reframing it. A stronger move would have been to acknowledge the gap briefly and pivot to confirming the Powerwall interest without dwelling on the miss.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep on how to absorb a process-credibility challenge without amplifying it. The honest admission is better than a dodge, but the rep could have compressed it: 'You're right — I've got it now. You were looking at the Powerwall. Let me make sure I get you the right information.' The goal is to close the credibility gap, not explain it.

quote: The only requirement to qualify is that you have a credit score of 6 50 or higher.
stewart_read: The rep states the credit threshold as 650, not 670. This matches the drift already flagged in the schema from SESSION18_fcbf33c1. The discrepancy matters here because Wayne's response ('I hope so. Yeah. By the time it gets there.') is hedged — if the actual threshold is 670 and Wayne is borderline, the rep may have inadvertently qualified someone who won't clear the closer's check.
coaching_implication: Manager needs to resolve the 650 vs. 670 threshold question with Kenny before this becomes a pattern of false qualifications. Flag this call as a second data point on the drift. Until resolved, coach reps to use the conservative number (670) to avoid setting up appointments that disqualify at the closer stage.

quote: I hope so. Yeah. By the time it gets there.
stewart_read: Wayne's credit self-assessment is explicitly conditional and forward-looking — 'by the time it gets there' implies his score may not currently be at threshold. The rep accepted this with 'Sounds good' and moved on without probing. This is a qualification gap that could surface as a disqualification at the closer stage, costing the rep a sit they won't get paid on.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep heard the hedge and chose not to probe because pushing on credit early in the call risked losing the booking momentum. Rejected — the rep's 'Sounds good' response and immediate pivot to the next question shows no awareness of the hedge, not a deliberate choice to defer.
coaching_implication: When a customer hedges on credit ('I hope so,' 'probably,' 'I think so'), the rep needs one follow-up probe: 'Do you have a rough sense of where you're at?' This isn't a DQ conversation — it's a flag-for-closer conversation. The rep should note the ambiguity in the handoff, not paper over it.

stewart_read: The rep captures the utility (Dominion) and the bill amount ($300) at 02:06-02:12, then responds with 'Sounds good' and moves directly to scheduling. The bill is captured as data and immediately dropped — no acknowledgment of what $300/month means for a Powerwall-only customer, no curiosity created around what Wayne is trying to solve. For a Powerwall lead specifically, the bill anchor is the setup for the usage-sizing conversation the closer will need to have.
coaching_implication: Even a single sentence bridges the bill capture to the appointment value: 'At $300 a month with Dominion, the specialist will want to look at your usage pattern to size the Powerwall correctly — that's exactly what tomorrow's call is for.' The rep doesn't need to sell anything; they need to connect the data point to the reason the appointment matters.

quote: No. I send you an I'll send I don't I don't do a lot with text. There's too many Okay. Links that are getting crazy. So if you have an email address or something, that'd be great.
stewart_read: Wayne's explanation for avoiding texts — 'too many links that are getting crazy' — is a scam-anxiety signal embedded in a channel preference. The rep correctly pivots to email, but treats this as a logistics problem rather than a trust signal. Wayne is telling the rep he's been targeted by phishing or spam links; the rep's response ('Yeah. For sure.') acknowledges the preference but misses the trust moment entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have read this as a simple channel preference and made the correct operational pivot. Reactive read holds — the scam-anxiety subtext is audible in Wayne's phrasing ('getting crazy') and warranted a brief acknowledgment before the logistics pivot.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep to register the anxiety before solving the logistics: 'Totally understand — there's a lot of sketchy stuff out there. I'll send it to your email, and it'll come from an ION address so you know it's us.' One sentence. It closes the trust gap Wayne just opened and makes the email more likely to be opened rather than filtered.

quote: K. Awesome. Well, I'll send you that text. And then if you could get that or sorry. Email. I'll send you that email. If you could get that sent over by today just so we can have that ready for you, by tomorrow, that'd be perfect.
stewart_read: The rep closes the call without completing the button-up protocol. Missing elements: no one-hour block stated, no 'not driving / at a computer' anchor, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no text-followup promise with a tieback to Wayne's specific interest. The rep also self-corrects mid-sentence ('I'll send you that text... sorry. Email.') — a small credibility nick after Wayne explicitly said he doesn't do texts. The bill-collection ask is present but the appointment confirmation scaffolding is absent.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep kept the close short because Wayne had already shown mild irritation and a compressed close reduces friction. Rejected — the missing button-up elements (computer anchor, verbal handshake) are no-show risk factors regardless of customer tone, and their omission is a protocol gap, not a deliberate de-escalation choice.
coaching_implication: Pull the button_up checklist against this call in the 1-on-1. The rep got the time and date confirmed but skipped the elements that predict show rate. The 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/not-driving anchor take 15 seconds combined. Run the rep through the full button-up sequence on a mock call before their next shift.

quote: Do you have panels on the home already?
stewart_read: This is a required orphaned-flow qualification check — the rep needs to confirm whether Wayne has an existing solar system before proceeding with a Powerwall-only conversation. The question is protocol-correct. It is included here because in context it reads as a third solar-adjacent probe to a customer who has already corrected the rep twice, and Wayne's flat 'No. I'm not doing any panels.' response shows he heard it that way. The rep's follow-up ('Oh, okay. Gotcha. So you just want the Powerwall?') lands the correct reframe.
coaching_implication: The question itself is correct. The coaching opportunity is in the framing: 'Just to make sure I set this up right for you — do you have any existing solar panels on the home?' positions the question as serving Wayne rather than probing him again. Minor phrasing adjustment, meaningful given the call's opening deficit.
